items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: Which mood follows 'je souhaite que' (I wish that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after expressions of wish. SOUHAITER QUE, VOULOIR QUE, AIMER QUE all trigger subjunctive when there's a different subject. Note: espérer que typically takes indicative - a common irregularity.

items.correct: A verb in the subjunctive mood.
items.why: Students use infinitive after impersonal expressions. 'Il faut que tu PARTES' (subjunctive), not 'il faut que tu partir'. The que triggers subjunctive in the following clause.

items.q: Which auxiliary does 'se marier' take in the passé composé?
items.why: It's être. “avoir” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: blended family. “nuclear family” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the PACS introduced in France?
items.why: Exact answer: 1999. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
